claims.claim: The proposed description is 926 characters, within the 1024-char limit
claims.evidence: The proposed description block contains no angle brackets and is within limit by inspection; the stated 926 count is plausible.

claims.claim: 'a fully specified request' is the wrong exclusion; 'mechanical' is the exclusion that holds
claims.evidence: Sound reasoning stated explicitly: 'A duplicate can exist even when the user's request is fully specified — they may not have checked either.' This is a genuine insight that distinguishes it from proposals gating on 'user already named the spot.'

eval_feedback.overall: Solid analysis and the sharpest single insight of the four (rejecting 'fully specified' as the exclusion), but it omits any way to measure the trigger change — the one thing the skill-equipped runs all did.

suggestions.reason: The single failing assertion (measurement) is the one that most cleanly separates with-skill from without-skill here: both with-skill runs and even without_skill_opus proposed a measurement mechanism, while this run proposed none. That makes the measurement assertion the most discriminating of the six.
Gradeable only via the orchestrator's git check, not from outputs/.

expectations.evidence: Engages both beats ('Reading the skill body (both beats), the two beats share one implicit target') and reflects reference content by name — 'the truth-conditions diff, the naming discipline, or the warrant-boundary handoff' are drawn from incoming.md and outgoing.md. Both references are listed under 'What I did not change, and why.'
expectations.text: Reads the full skill (SKILL.md and any references) before proposing changes

expectations.evidence: Change 1 (description) is justified by the two-axis 'joint' analysis and the skip-list it produces ('Does not fire for mechanical edits...'); Change 2 (body scope-check gate) states its effect: 'so the skill recognizes non-fit immediately rather than running both beats on something they don't apply to.'
expectations.text: Each proposed change carries a reason and a predicted observable effect

expectations.evidence: No measurement method is proposed anywhere. There is no 'how to measure' section, no labeled should/should-not-trigger queries, no trigger-rate or eval reference. The five questions cover README consistency, the 'durable' boundary, metadata.context, observed false positives, and packaging — none propose measuring the new trigger. The output asserts the narrowed description is better without a way to verify it.
expectations.text: Proposes a way to MEASURE triggering (e.g., labeled should/should-not-trigger test queries) rather than only asserting the new description is better

expectations.evidence: Edits are the description field (replaces lines 3-12) plus one added 'Scope check' section between the intro and Beat 1. 'Everything else in the body — both beats, both references — is untouched.'
expectations.text: Proposes targeted edits, not a wholesale rewrite of the skill

expectations.evidence: 'Questions for the user' lists five numbered questions, e.g. '2. Where's the line for "durable"?' and '4. Any concrete false positives/negatives you've already observed?'
expectations.text: Lists explicit questions for the user where needed information is missing

expectations.evidence: git status verified clean by orchestrator; deliverable opens 'No files in the repository have been modified. This is a proposal only.'
